cards.back: It defines the human-readable string representation of an object. Python calls it automatically when you pass an object to print() or str(). It should return a nicely formatted string - never print directly.
When you call print(obj), str(obj), or use an object in an f-string like f"{obj}". You define it once and Python handles the rest - no separate print function needed.
cards.code:
game = GameMap(hidden)
print(game)         # calls game.__str__()
f"Map: {game}"     # also calls __str__

cards.back: Return - always. __str__ must return a string. If you print inside it, print() can't do anything useful with the return value (None). Returning gives callers flexibility: they can print it, log it, store it, or embed it in another string.

It defines a developer-facing representation of an object - typically enough detail to understand or reconstruct the object's state. Shown in the REPL, debuggers, and log output. Should be unambiguous.
In the interactive Python REPL when you evaluate an expression, when using repr(obj), and when an object appears inside a container like a list printed with print([obj]).

__str__ is for humans - readable and pretty. __repr__ is for developers - precise and unambiguous. If only one is defined, Python falls back to __repr__ when __str__ is needed, but not the other way around.

Methods with double underscore prefix and suffix (e.g. __init__, __str__, __repr__). Also called 'magic methods' or 'special methods'. Python calls them automatically in specific situations - you define them, Python decides when to invoke them.

It combines all strings in the lines list into one string, with a newline character between each pair. Much more efficient than building a string with += in a loop because join() allocates memory only once.

cards.front: Why use a list + join() instead of string concatenation in __str__?
cards.back: String concatenation (+=) creates a new string object every time, which is slow for many lines. Building a list of strings and calling join() at the end allocates memory exactly once. It's faster and more Pythonic.

cards.front: What happens if you define __str__ but not __repr__?
cards.back: print(obj) uses your __str__. repr(obj) and the REPL fall back to the default __repr__, which shows something like <GameMap object at 0x10a2b3c40> - not very informative. Define both when debugging matters.

cards.front: What happens if you define neither __str__ nor __repr__?
cards.back: Python uses the inherited default from object, which shows the class name and memory address: <GameMap object at 0x10a2b3c40>. Functional but useless for debugging.

cards.front: How do you call __str__ explicitly (two ways)?
cards.back:
str(obj) calls __str__ and returns the result.
obj.__str__() also works but is not recommended - calling dunder methods directly is considered bad style. Use the built-in function or just print(obj).

Enough information to understand the object's current state - class name, key attributes, and anything that distinguishes this instance. A good rule: if you read __repr__ output in a log, you should understand what the object is and what it was doing.

cards.front: What does f"GameMap({self.rows}x{self.cols})" produce if rows=5, cols=5?
cards.back: The string 'GameMap(5x5)'. The f-string evaluates self.rows and self.cols and inserts their values. This is a typical __repr__ format: class name followed by key state in parentheses.
str(obj) calls obj.__str__(). print(obj) also calls __str__ internally before printing the result.
repr(obj) calls obj.__repr__(). The interactive REPL also calls __repr__ when it displays the result of an expression.

cards.front: Can __str__ call other methods of the same class?
cards.back: Yes - self is available inside __str__ just like any other method. You can call self.total_minutes(), self.unique_activities(), or any other method to build the output string.
The string '☁   ☁   · ' - each element joined with two spaces between them. join() inserts the separator between elements, not after the last one.
It produces pairs of (index, row) for each row in self.revealed, so you can use the row number as a label without maintaining a manual counter.
cards.code:
for r, row in enumerate(self.revealed):
    line = f"r{r}  " + "  ".join(row)

cards.front: Why is it better to return from __str__ than to print inside it?
cards.back: Returning makes __str__ composable - the caller decides what to do with the string. It can be printed, logged, written to a file, or embedded in a larger string. Printing inside the method takes that control away from the caller.

It creates one final string by joining the header (a single string) with all the row strings, separated by newlines. The + operator here concatenates two lists before join() combines their elements into one string.

cards.front: If GameMap has both __str__ and __repr__, which one does print() use?
cards.back: print() uses __str__. Only if __str__ is not defined does Python fall back to __repr__.
